question_en: What is the largest planet in the solar system?
context_en: Jupiter has a mass 318 times that of Earth. It has at least 95 known moons; Ganymede is the largest in the solar system.

question_en: Which galaxy contains our solar system?
context_en: The Milky Way is a barred spiral galaxy about 100,000 light-years in diameter. It contains between 100 and 400 billion stars.

question_en: Who was the first human to walk on the Moon?
context_en: Neil Armstrong walked on the Moon on July 20, 1969, on the Apollo 11 mission. Along with Aldrin, he stayed ~21.5 h on the surface.

question_en: How many planets does the solar system have (officially)?
context_en: Since 2006, Pluto is a dwarf planet. There remain 8 official planets: Mercury, Venus, Earth, Mars, Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus and Neptune.

question_en: Which planet is closest to the Sun?
context_en: Mercury is the closest to the Sun (~58 M km). It has a year of only 88 Earth days.

question_en: At what speed does light travel in a vacuum?
context_en: Light travels at 299,792,458 m/s in a vacuum. It is a fundamental constant ('c') in the theory of relativity.

question_en: Which star is the center of our solar system?
context_en: The Sun is a yellow dwarf star (G2V) about 4.6 billion years old. It contains 99.86% of the solar system's mass.

question_en: How many moons does Mars have?
context_en: Mars has 2 moons: Phobos and Deimos. They are small and irregular, probably captured asteroids.

question_en: Which is the closest star to the Sun?
context_en: Proxima Centauri (part of the Alpha Centauri system) is ~4.24 light-years away. It is a red dwarf.

question_en: Which planet is known as the 'red planet'?
context_en: Mars looks reddish because of the iron oxide on its surface. The Romans associated it with the god of war.

question_en: Approximately how old is the universe?
context_en: The universe is ~13.8 billion years old, according to measurements of the cosmic microwave background (CMB).

question_en: What kind of object is a black hole?
context_en: A black hole is a region of spacetime with gravity so intense that not even light escapes. Its boundary is the event horizon.

question_en: Who proposed the heliocentric model?
context_en: Nicolaus Copernicus published 'De revolutionibus orbium coelestium' in 1543. It places the Sun (not Earth) at the center of the system.

question_en: Which planet has the most visible rings?
context_en: Saturn has the most visible rings, made of billions of particles of ice and rock. All the gas giants have rings.

question_en: How long does the Earth take to rotate on its axis?
context_en: The Earth completes one rotation in ~24 h (solar day). The sidereal day is 23 h 56 min 4 s.

question_en: How long does sunlight take to reach the Earth?
context_en: Sunlight takes ~8 minutes and 20 seconds to travel the 150 M km average between the Sun and Earth (1 AU).

question_en: What is Jupiter's 'Great Red Spot'?
context_en: The Great Red Spot is a giant anticyclonic storm on Jupiter, observed for at least 350 years. It is shrinking in size.

question_en: Which constellation contains the North Star?
context_en: The North Star (Polaris) is in Ursa Minor. It marks approximately the north celestial pole.

question_en: Which is the hottest planet in the solar system?
context_en: Venus has surface temperatures of ~470 °C, hotter than Mercury. Its dense CO₂ atmosphere traps heat (extreme greenhouse effect).

question_en: What is the closest galaxy to the Milky Way?
context_en: Andromeda (M31) is the closest spiral galaxy, at 2.5 million light-years. It will collide with the Milky Way in ~4.5 billion years.

question_en: Which space telescope succeeded the Hubble?
context_en: The James Webb Space Telescope was launched in 2021. It observes mainly in the infrared, complementing (not replacing) the Hubble.

question_en: What is the unit used for distances between stars?
context_en: The light-year is the distance light travels in one year: ~9.46 trillion km. The parsec (3.26 light-years) is also used.

question_en: What is the name of the theory of the origin of the universe?
context_en: The Big Bang proposes that the universe expanded from a dense, hot state ~13.8 billion years ago.

question_en: What are shooting stars called?
context_en: Meteors are luminous streaks of small bodies that burn up on entering the atmosphere. If they survive impact, they are meteorites.

question_en: Which NASA spacecraft reached Pluto in 2015?
context_en: New Horizons flew by Pluto on July 14, 2015, sending the first detailed images of this icy world.

question_en: What phase is the Moon in when it cannot be seen from Earth?
context_en: At new moon, the illuminated side faces the Sun and we do not see the Moon's night side. This is when solar eclipses occur.

question_en: How many moons does the Earth have?
context_en: The Earth has a single permanent natural moon. It occasionally captures small asteroids temporarily ('minimoon').

question_en: Who formulated the three laws of planetary motion?
context_en: Johannes Kepler published his 3 laws between 1609 and 1619, based on data from Tycho Brahe. They describe elliptical orbits.

question_en: Which is the largest natural satellite in the solar system?
context_en: Ganymede (a moon of Jupiter) is the largest satellite, even larger than Mercury. It is 5,268 km in diameter.

question_en: What type of star is the Sun?
context_en: The Sun is a yellow dwarf of class G2V, on the main sequence. It has about 5 billion years left before becoming a red giant.

question_en: Which planet spins on its side?
context_en: Uranus spins with its axis tilted 98°. It was possibly struck by an Earth-sized object billions of years ago.

question_en: What is the name of the stellar atmosphere visible during an eclipse?
context_en: The solar corona is the Sun's outer atmosphere, visible during a total eclipse. Its temperature exceeds 1 M K.

question_en: Who discovered the principle of universal gravitation?
context_en: Isaac Newton published the law of universal gravitation in 'Principia' (1687). Any pair of masses attract with F=Gm₁m₂/r².

question_en: What phenomenon occurs when the Moon comes between the Sun and the Earth?
context_en: In a solar eclipse, the Moon casts a shadow on Earth. Totality is only seen from the umbra zone (a narrow band).

question_en: In what year did Apollo 11 land on the Moon?
context_en: Apollo 11 landed on July 20, 1969. There were 6 successful crewed missions (Apollo 11, 12, 14, 15, 16 and 17).

question_en: Which is the farthest planet (no longer counting Pluto)?
context_en: Neptune is the farthest planet from the Sun (~4.5 billion km). Pluto has been a dwarf planet since 2006.

question_en: What is a pulsar?
options_en: A neutron star that emits pulses
context_en: A pulsar is a rotating neutron star that emits beams of electromagnetic radiation. Discovered by Jocelyn Bell in 1967.

question_en: Which telescope discovered thousands of galaxies?
context_en: The Hubble (launched in 1990) revolutionized astronomy: it measured the expansion of the universe, observed remote galaxies and refined the age of the cosmos.

question_en: What is the approximate diameter of the Sun?
context_en: The Sun is ~1,392,000 km in diameter, about 109 times that of Earth. It contains 99.86% of the solar system's mass.

question_en: What is a massive stellar explosion called?
context_en: A supernova is the explosion of a massive star at the end of its life. It shines like an entire galaxy for weeks.

question_en: What is the mean Earth-Moon distance?
context_en: The Moon is 384,400 km away on average. The distance varies slightly due to its elliptical orbit (perigee and apogee).

question_en: Which famous comet will be seen again in 2061?
context_en: Halley's Comet passes near Earth every 75-76 years. Last seen in 1986, next visit in 2061.

question_en: What is the approximate temperature of the Sun's surface?
context_en: The Sun's photosphere is at ~5,500 °C. The corona, paradoxically, is much hotter (>1 M °C).

question_en: What phenomenon explains why galaxies move away from each other?
context_en: The expansion of the universe, discovered by Hubble in 1929. The distance between galaxies increases; it is measured by the Hubble constant.

question_en: What is dark energy?
options_en: A force that accelerates the expansion
context_en: Dark energy represents ~68% of the universe and is thought to cause its accelerated expansion, discovered in 1998.

question_en: In which constellation is the Orion Nebula?
context_en: The Orion Nebula (M42) is visible to the naked eye in Orion's 'sword'. It is a star-forming region.

question_en: Who discovered the four largest moons of Jupiter?
context_en: Galileo discovered Io, Europa, Ganymede and Callisto in 1610 with his telescope. They are called the 'Galilean moons' in his honor.

question_en: What color is the light of the hottest stars?
context_en: The hottest stars are blue (tens of thousands of K). The temperature order: red, orange, yellow, white and blue.

question_en: Which planet has a day longer than its year?
context_en: Venus rotates very slowly: a solar day lasts 117 Earth days, while its year is 225 days. It also rotates opposite to the rest.

question_en: In what year was Sputnik 1, the first artificial satellite, launched?
context_en: The USSR launched Sputnik 1 on October 4, 1957, inaugurating the space age and the space race with the USA.

question_en: How many moons does Jupiter have (officially confirmed, approx.)?
context_en: Jupiter has more than 90 confirmed moons (>95 as of 2024). The four largest (Galilean) are Io, Europa, Ganymede and Callisto.

question_en: Which is Jupiter's largest moon?
context_en: Ganymede is the largest moon in the solar system (even larger than Mercury).

question_en: Which moon of Jupiter could harbor a subsurface ocean?
context_en: Europa, a moon of Jupiter, has an ice crust beneath which an ocean of liquid water is believed to exist.

question_en: Which moon of Saturn has a dense atmosphere?
context_en: Titan is the only moon in the solar system with a dense atmosphere (denser than Earth's). It has lakes of liquid methane.

question_en: Which moon of Saturn ejects geysers of water?
context_en: Enceladus has geysers at the south pole that eject water from the subsurface ocean into space.

question_en: How many moons does Saturn have (confirmed, approx.)?
context_en: Saturn has more than 140 confirmed moons (2024), making it the planet with the most known satellites.

question_en: How many moons does Uranus have?
context_en: Uranus has 27 known moons. The five largest are Miranda, Ariel, Umbriel, Titania and Oberon.

question_en: How many moons does Neptune have?
context_en: Neptune has 14 known moons. The largest is Triton, which orbits in a retrograde direction.

question_en: Which is the largest moon of Neptune?
context_en: Triton is the largest moon of Neptune and the seventh in the solar system. It has nitrogen volcanoes.

question_en: How many planets does the solar system have?
context_en: The solar system has 8 planets officially, since Pluto was reclassified as a dwarf planet in 2006.

question_en: When did Pluto stop being considered a planet?
context_en: The International Astronomical Union reclassified Pluto as a 'dwarf planet' in August 2006.

question_en: How many dwarf planets are there officially?
context_en: The IAU recognizes 5 dwarf planets: Ceres, Pluto, Haumea, Makemake and Eris.

question_en: Which dwarf planet is in the asteroid belt?
context_en: Ceres, the dwarf planet closest to the Sun, is in the asteroid belt between Mars and Jupiter.

question_en: Which dwarf planet is larger than Pluto?
context_en: Eris (discovered in 2005) is more massive than Pluto although slightly smaller in diameter. Its discovery triggered the 'demotion' of Pluto.

question_en: How long does Mercury take to orbit the Sun?
context_en: Mercury has the shortest year in the solar system: 88 Earth days.

question_en: How long does Venus take to orbit the Sun?
context_en: Venus takes about 225 Earth days to orbit the Sun.

question_en: How long does Mars take to orbit the Sun?
context_en: Mars takes about 687 Earth days (1.88 years) to orbit the Sun.

question_en: How long does Jupiter take to orbit the Sun?
context_en: Jupiter takes about 12 Earth years to orbit the Sun.

question_en: How long does Saturn take to orbit the Sun?
context_en: Saturn takes about 29.5 Earth years to orbit the Sun.

question_en: How long does Uranus take to orbit the Sun?
context_en: Uranus takes about 84 Earth years to orbit the Sun.

question_en: How long does Neptune take to orbit the Sun?
context_en: Neptune takes about 165 Earth years to orbit the Sun.

question_en: Which is the natural satellite of the Earth?
context_en: The Moon is the only natural satellite of the Earth. Its mean distance is 384,400 km.

question_en: What are the moons of Mars called?
context_en: Phobos and Deimos are the two small moons of Mars, probably captured asteroids.

question_en: What is the approximate temperature of Venus?
context_en: Venus has a surface temperature of about 465 °C due to the intense greenhouse effect of its CO₂ atmosphere.

question_en: Why is Venus the hottest planet?
options_en: It is closer to the Sun
context_en: Although Mercury is closer to the Sun, Venus is hotter because of the extreme greenhouse effect of its CO₂ atmosphere.

question_en: Which is the closest planet to the Earth?
context_en: Venus is the closest planet to the Earth. Its minimum distance is about 38 million km.

question_en: What color is Mars in the night sky?
context_en: Mars looks reddish in the sky. The color is due to iron oxide (ferric oxide) on its surface.

question_en: Which is the tallest mountain in the solar system?
context_en: Olympus Mons, on Mars, is the tallest volcano in the solar system: ~22 km high.

question_en: Which planet has the most intense storms, lasting centuries?
context_en: Jupiter's Great Red Spot is an anticyclonic storm that has lasted at least 350 years.

question_en: What is a meteorite?
options_en: A rock that reaches the Earth's surface
context_en: A meteorite is a rocky fragment from space that survives passage through the atmosphere and impacts the Earth.

question_en: What is an asteroid?
options_en: A small rocky body that orbits the Sun
context_en: Asteroids are small rocky bodies that orbit the Sun, mainly between Mars and Jupiter.

question_en: What is a comet?
options_en: A body of ice and dust that produces a tail
context_en: A comet is a body made mainly of ice and dust. As it approaches the Sun, it sublimates, forming a coma and tail.

question_en: What is the name of the comet that appears roughly every 76 years?
context_en: Halley's Comet orbits the Sun about every 76 years. Its last visit was in 1986 and it will return in 2061.

question_en: Which is the closest galaxy to the Milky Way?
context_en: The Canis Major Dwarf galaxy is about 25,000 light-years away. Andromeda is the nearest large spiral galaxy, at 2.5 M light-years.

question_en: When will the Milky Way collide with Andromeda (prediction)?
context_en: The Milky Way and Andromeda are predicted to collide in about 4.5 billion years.

question_en: How many light-years is the diameter of the Milky Way?
context_en: The Milky Way is about 100,000 light-years in diameter and has about 100-400 billion stars.

question_en: Which is the most famous nebula, visible to the naked eye?
context_en: The Orion Nebula (M42), in the constellation of Orion, is visible to the naked eye in Orion's 'belt'.

question_en: Which space telescope was launched in 1990?
context_en: The Hubble Space Telescope was launched by NASA on April 24, 1990.

question_en: Which space telescope was launched in 2021?
context_en: The James Webb telescope was launched on December 25, 2021. It began scientific operations in July 2022.

question_en: At what distance does the James Webb telescope operate?
options_en: At the L2 point (~1.5 M km)
context_en: The James Webb telescope operates at the Lagrange point 2 (L2), about 1.5 million km from Earth.

question_en: How many constellations does the IAU recognize?
context_en: The International Astronomical Union officially recognizes 88 constellations since 1928.

question_en: Which constellation contains the North Star?
context_en: The North Star (Polaris) is in the constellation of Ursa Minor. It indicates celestial north.

question_en: Which is the brightest star in the night sky?
context_en: Sirius, in the constellation of Canis Major, is the brightest star in the night sky (magnitude -1.46).

question_en: In which constellation is Sirius?
context_en: Sirius is in the constellation of Canis Major. It is the 'dog star'.

question_en: Which constellation is shaped like a 'W'?
context_en: Cassiopeia, in the northern sky, is shaped like a 'W' or 'M'. It is circumpolar for mid-latitudes of the northern hemisphere.

question_en: Which constellation has three aligned stars in its 'belt'?
context_en: Orion, the hunter, is recognized by the three aligned stars of his belt: Alnitak, Alnilam and Mintaka.

question_en: Which belt is located between Mars and Jupiter?
context_en: The main asteroid belt is between the orbits of Mars and Jupiter.

question_en: What is the Kuiper Belt?
options_en: A ring of icy bodies beyond Neptune
context_en: The Kuiper Belt is a region beyond Neptune with icy bodies, including Pluto.

question_en: What is the Oort cloud?
options_en: A distant sphere of cometary objects
context_en: The Oort Cloud is a theoretical sphere of icy objects (up to ~100,000 AU from the Sun). The source of long-period comets.

question_en: Which unit measures the distance between the Earth and the Sun?
context_en: 1 Astronomical Unit (AU) = ~149.6 million km, the mean Earth-Sun distance.

question_en: Approximately how many km is a light-year?
context_en: A light-year is equivalent to approximately 9.46 trillion (10¹²) kilometers.

question_en: At what speed is light measured in km/s?
context_en: The speed of light in a vacuum is approximately 300,000 km/s (exactly 299,792.458 km/s).

question_en: What is the name of the theory that explains the origin of the universe?
context_en: The Big Bang theory proposes that the universe originated about 13.8 billion years ago from a dense, hot state.

question_en: How many years ago did the Big Bang occur?
context_en: According to the best estimates, the Big Bang occurred ~13.8 billion years ago.

question_en: What makes up approx. 27% of the universe according to current cosmology?
context_en: Dark matter makes up approximately 27% of the universe. Ordinary matter only 5%. The rest (68%) is dark energy.

question_en: What makes up approx. 68% of the universe?
context_en: Dark energy makes up ~68% of the universe. It is responsible for the accelerated expansion of the universe.

question_en: Who proposed the expansion of the universe?
context_en: Edwin Hubble observed in 1929 that galaxies are moving away, evidencing the expansion of the universe. Georges Lemaître had proposed it theoretically.

question_en: Which phenomenon demonstrates the expansion of the universe?
context_en: The redshift of light from distant galaxies demonstrates that they are moving away from us.

question_en: Which event is the proof of the Big Bang?
context_en: The cosmic microwave background (CMB), discovered in 1964, is the 'residual light' of the Big Bang.

question_en: What type of star is our Sun?
context_en: The Sun is a yellow dwarf star of spectral type G2V.

question_en: What is the approximate age of the Sun?
context_en: The Sun is about 4.6 billion years old. It formed at the same time as the rest of the solar system.

question_en: How much longer will the Sun live as a stable star (approx.)?
context_en: The Sun has about 5 billion more years as a main-sequence star before becoming a red giant.

question_en: What is a supernova?
context_en: A supernova is the massive explosion at the end of the life of a very massive star or a binary system with a white dwarf.

question_en: What remains after a supernova?
options_en: A neutron star or black hole
context_en: After a supernova, a neutron star or a black hole may remain (depending on the original mass).

question_en: What is a neutron star?
context_en: Neutron stars are ultra-dense stellar remnants. A teaspoonful would weigh billions of tons.

question_en: What is a quasar?
context_en: Quasars are active galactic nuclei with supermassive black holes at their center, extremely bright.

question_en: Who predicted the existence of black holes (mathematically)?
context_en: Karl Schwarzschild found in 1916 the first solution of Einstein's equations that predicted black holes.

question_en: Who researched the radiation of black holes?
context_en: Stephen Hawking predicted in 1974 that black holes emit radiation ('Hawking radiation') and can evaporate.

question_en: What is the event horizon of a black hole?
context_en: The event horizon is the boundary where the escape velocity equals c. Once crossed, nothing (not even light) can leave.

question_en: What is Sagittarius A*?
options_en: The supermassive black hole at the center of the Milky Way
context_en: Sgr A* is the supermassive black hole at the center of the Milky Way, with 4.3 million solar masses.

question_en: Which planet has the longest day in the solar system?
context_en: Venus has a day (rotation) of 243 Earth days, longer than its year (225 days).

question_en: In which direction does Venus rotate?
context_en: Venus rotates in a retrograde direction (east to west), unlike most planets in the solar system.

question_en: In which direction does Uranus rotate?
context_en: Uranus has its rotation axis tilted ~98°, so it rotates 'on its side' relative to the plane of its orbit.

question_en: What is the summer solstice (northern hemisphere)?
context_en: The summer solstice in the northern hemisphere occurs around June 21, the longest day of the year.

question_en: When does the spring equinox occur (northern hemisphere)?
context_en: The spring equinox in the northern hemisphere occurs around March 20-21. Day and night are approximately equal.

question_en: What phenomenon makes us always see the same face of the Moon?
context_en: The Moon is tidally locked with the Earth: its rotation period matches its orbital period, always showing the same face.

question_en: How long does the Moon take to orbit the Earth?
context_en: The Moon takes about 27.3 days to orbit the Earth (sidereal month). The synodic month (between new moons) is ~29.5 days.

question_en: What happens during a solar eclipse?
options_en: The Moon comes between the Sun and the Earth
The Earth comes between the Moon and the Sun
context_en: A solar eclipse occurs when the Moon comes between the Sun and the Earth, hiding it.

question_en: What happens during a lunar eclipse?
options_en: The Earth comes between the Sun and the Moon
context_en: A lunar eclipse occurs when the Earth comes between the Sun and the Moon, casting its shadow over it.

question_en: Who was the first cosmonaut?
context_en: Yuri Gagarin (USSR) was the first human to travel into space on April 12, 1961, aboard the Vostok 1 spacecraft.

question_en: Who was the first woman in space?
context_en: Valentina Tereshkova (USSR) was the first woman in space on June 16, 1963.

question_en: In what year did humans first walk on the Moon?
context_en: Neil Armstrong and Buzz Aldrin walked on the Moon on July 20, 1969, on the Apollo 11 mission.

question_en: How many Apollo missions landed on the Moon?
context_en: Six Apollo missions landed on the Moon: Apollo 11, 12, 14, 15, 16 and 17 (between 1969 and 1972). Number 13 failed.

question_en: When was the last crewed Apollo mission to the Moon?
context_en: The last crewed mission to the Moon was Apollo 17 in December 1972, with Gene Cernan as the last human on the Moon.

question_en: Which space agency runs the Artemis program?
context_en: NASA's Artemis program aims to bring humans back to the Moon in 2025-2026, including the first woman.

question_en: What is the ISS?
context_en: The International Space Station (ISS) has orbited the Earth since 1998. A cooperation between NASA, Roscosmos, ESA, JAXA and CSA.

question_en: At what altitude does the ISS orbit approximately?
context_en: The ISS orbits at about 400 km altitude. It circles the Earth every ~90 minutes.

question_en: Which mission reached Pluto in 2015?
context_en: NASA's New Horizons probe flew by Pluto on July 14, 2015.

question_en: Which probe explored Saturn until 2017?
context_en: The Cassini-Huygens mission orbited Saturn from 2004 to 2017. Huygens landed on Titan in 2005.

question_en: Which probe currently explores Jupiter?
context_en: NASA's Juno probe, launched in 2011, has orbited Jupiter since 2016.

question_en: Which mission reached Mars with the Perseverance rover?
context_en: The Mars 2020 mission carried the Perseverance rover, which landed on Mars on February 18, 2021.

question_en: Which small helicopter explored Mars?
context_en: Ingenuity, NASA's helicopter, arrived on Mars with Perseverance in 2021. The first powered flight on another planet.

question_en: Which private company has achieved the most space launches recently?
context_en: SpaceX, founded by Elon Musk, leads private space launches with its Falcon 9 rocket.

question_en: Which reusable rocket made SpaceX famous?
context_en: Falcon 9 was the first orbital rocket with a reusable stage. Its first successful landing was in December 2015.

question_en: What is the name of SpaceX's rocket for Mars?
context_en: Starship is SpaceX's heavy rocket, designed for missions to the Moon, Mars and beyond. In testing since 2023.

question_en: What waves do pulsars emit?
context_en: Pulsars are neutron stars that emit beams of radio waves (and sometimes others) at regular intervals.

question_en: Who discovered pulsars?
context_en: Jocelyn Bell Burnell discovered the first pulsar in 1967 as a doctoral student at Cambridge.

question_en: How many galaxies are estimated to exist in the observable universe?
context_en: The observable universe is estimated to contain approximately 2 trillion (10¹²) galaxies.

question_en: What is an exoplanet?
options_en: A planet outside the solar system
context_en: An exoplanet is a planet that orbits a star other than the Sun. More than 5,500 exoplanets have been confirmed.

question_en: What is the Goldilocks zone?
options_en: The habitable zone of a star
context_en: The habitable or Goldilocks zone is the region around a star where liquid water could exist on the surface of a planet.

question_en: Which telescope has confirmed thousands of exoplanets?
context_en: NASA's Kepler (2009-2018) and TESS (since 2018) telescopes have confirmed thousands of exoplanets.

question_en: What is zodiacal light?
context_en: Zodiacal light is a faint glow produced by the scattering of sunlight by dust in the plane of the solar system.

question_en: What phenomenon creates the aurora borealis?
context_en: Auroras occur when charged particles from the solar wind collide with the atmosphere, guided by the Earth's magnetic field.

question_en: Who defined the laws of planetary motion?
context_en: Johannes Kepler formulated his three laws of planetary motion between 1609 and 1619.

question_en: Who unified terrestrial and celestial laws with gravity?
context_en: Isaac Newton unified terrestrial and celestial laws in his 'Principia' (1687) with the law of universal gravitation.

question_en: Who revolutionized our understanding of gravity with relativity?
context_en: Albert Einstein published the theory of general relativity in 1915, describing gravity as the curvature of spacetime.
